revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石崎奉燈祭]]
{{Image requested|main festival|date=December 2007|cat=Nanao}} '''Ishizaki Hōtō Festival''' (石崎奉燈祭, ''Issaki Hōtō Matsuri'')<ref name="notokiriko">''Nihon Isan Noto no Kiriko Matsuri,'' Hokuriku Research Institute, [[Hokkoku Shimbun]], June 20, 2016, ISBN 978-4-8330-2068-8, p.74</ref><ref>[https://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/kiriko/jp/kiriko/issaki.php Noto no Kiriko Matsuri - Ishizaki Hōtō Matsuri]</ref> is a [[Noto Kiriko Matsuri|Kiriko Festival]] held annually on the first Saturday of August in {{ill|Ishizaki|en|Ishizaki|qid=Q26203496|lt=Ishizaki|1=Ishizaki}}, {{ill|Nanao|en|Nanao|qid=Q1214984|lt=Nanao|1=Nanao}}, {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}. It is a dedication festival (''Hōnōsai'') of [[Ishizaki Hachiman Shrine|Ishizaki Hachiman Shrine]] and is considered one of the four major festivals of Nanao, along with the [[Seihakusai]], [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]], and [[Mukōda Fire Festival]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.nanao.lg.jp/kikaku-s/shise/sesaku/1sogokekaku/honpen/documents/kihonkeikaku_1_4_5.pdf|title=Basic Plan: Chapter 1, Section 4, Arts and Culture|accessdate=2024-08-04|publisher=Nanao City|page=88}}</ref>{{Refnest|group="Note"|text=The Seihakusai, [[Nanao Gion Festival]], and [[Nanao Port Festival]] are sometimes listed among Nanao's four major festivals.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://yamaguchi-ev.com/ishikawa-event/1692/|title=Ishikawa Events in May|accessdate=2024-08-04|publisher=Yamaguchi Corporation}}</ref>}} The festival includes a pre-event on the preceding Friday. While the district is named "Ishizaki," the festival name is pronounced "Issaki."<ref name="notokiriko" />
== Overview == The Ishizaki Hōtō Festival is part of the [[Noto Kiriko Matsuri|Kiriko Festivals]] in the region, which trace their origins to the [[Gion Matsuri]] of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. Historically, the Ishizaki district used to parade Gion-style floats. However, after several fires during the summer festival of Ishizaki Hachiman Shrine, a decision was made in {{ill|1889|en|1889|qid=Q7831|lt=1889|1=1889}} to adopt the use of Kiriko floats, which were seen as a safer alternative.
The highlight of the festival involves local fishermen carrying massive Kiriko floats, 13–15 meters tall and weighing about 2 tons, through the narrow streets of the fishing town. Accompanied by traditional flutes, drums, and gongs, the carriers shout "Sakkasai! Sakasassai! Iyasa Kasaa!" while parading and dancing energetically. In the evening, a [[Mikoshi]] procession and large Kiriko dances take place at the shrine's ''Otabisho''. Fireworks and illuminated Kiriko floats add to the festival's spectacle, which lasts well into the night. Before 1995, the festival was held on the old lunar calendar's Gion Festival date, June 15.
In {{ill|1997|en|1997|qid=Q2088|lt=1997|1=1997}}, the festival was selected as part of the [[Documentation of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties]] program.<ref>[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/161401 Cultural Heritage Online (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref> In {{ill|2015|en|2015|qid=Q2002|lt=2015|1=2015}}, it was also recognized as part of the {{ill|Japan Heritage|en|Japan Heritage|qid=Q18382798|lt=Japan Heritage|1=Japan Heritage}} "Lighting the Peninsula: Noto - Enthralling Kiriko Festivals."<ref>[http://www.bunka.go.jp/seisaku/bunkazai/nihon_isan/ichiran.html List of Certified Japan Heritage Sites (Agency for Cultural Affairs)]</ref>
The Ishizaki Hōtō Festival collaborates with the [[Nanao Gion Festival]], sharing personnel to ensure the success of both events.
== Kiriko (Hōtō) == Seven districts each present a pair of large and small Kiriko floats. The front of the Kiriko features auspicious Chinese characters written in bold calligraphy, while the back often displays heroic ''Musha-e'' (warrior pictures). The Kiriko floats are carried manually without wheels, making the large ones some of the heaviest portable floats in Japan. At night, the Kiriko are illuminated, creating a stunning visual display.
=== Participating Districts === Each district has its own colors and features: * '''East 1 District''': Team Color: Green; Inscription: ''Fish-Filled Bay.'' * '''East 2 District''': Team Color: Yellow; Inscription: ''Endless Bliss, Boundless Fortune.'' * '''East 3 District''': Team Color: Red; Inscription: ''Desire Tranquility.'' * '''East 4 District''': Team Color: Blue; Inscription: ''Wisdom, Benevolence, Courage.'' * '''West 1 District''': Team Color: White; Inscription: ''Soaring Silver Fish.'' * '''West 2 District''': Team Color: Pink; Inscription: ''Auspicious Clouds.'' * '''West 3 District''': Team Color: Purple; Inscription: ''Soaring Clouds.''
== Accidents == In 2001, a 22-year-old spectator tragically died after being crushed between a Kiriko float and a wall. While safety measures have been discussed, significant preventive actions have yet to be implemented.
== Festival Outreach == The Ishizaki Hōtō floats have been featured in several events outside their home district, such as the 1970 {{ill|Expo '70|en|Expo '70|qid=Q1119956|lt=Expo '70|1=Expo '70}} in Osaka and the 2015 [[Furusato Festival Tokyo]] at {{ill|Tokyo Dome|en|Tokyo Dome|qid=Q733748|lt=Tokyo Dome|1=Tokyo Dome}}.
== Related Museums == * '''[[Wakura Onsen Festival Hall|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall]]''': Located in Wakura Onsen, it displays full-scale Kiriko floats and celebrates Nanao's festival culture. * '''[[Wajima Kiriko Art Museum]]''': Located in {{ill|Wajima|en|Wajima|qid=Q241177|lt=Wajima|1=Wajima}}, this museum exhibits a variety of Kiriko floats from the Noto region, including those from Ishizaki.
== Notes == {{Reflist|group="Note"}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * [http://www.hot-ishikawa.jp/kiriko/jp/index.php Noto's Kiriko Festivals] * [http://www.notokiriko.jp/kiriko.html Noto Kiriko Matsuri Official Website]
{{translated page|ja|石崎奉燈祭|version=104551332|comment=Imported full ja history}}
